sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
प्रजहाति यदा कामान्सर्वान्पार्थ मनोगतान् ।
आत्मन्येवात्मना तुष्टः स्थितप्रज्ञस्तदोच्यते ॥ ५५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
prajahāti yadā kāmān sarvān pārtha mano-gatān |
ātmany evātmanā tuṣṭaḥ sthita-prajñas tadocyate || 55 ||

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.055 = MBh_06,024.055). Body text identical: 'prajahāti yadā kāmān sarvān pārtha manogatān | ātmanyevātmanā tuṣṭaḥ sthitaprajñastadocyate'. GRETIL writes the speaker tag 'śrībhagavānuvāca' on the prior line as one compound; modern editions break it into 'śrī-bhagavān uvāca'. Scribal-convention only; no semantic difference.

anvaya.meaning: gone / entered (in compound: 'that have entered the mind')

translations.verbatim_quote: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O Pārtha, when a man gives up all varieties of desire for sense gratification, which arise from mental concoction, and when his mind, thus purified, finds satisfaction in the self alone, then he is said to be in pure transcendental consciousness.

The Supreme Lord said: O Parth, when one discards all selfish desires and cravings of the senses that torment the mind, and becomes satisfied in the realization of the self, such a person is said to be transcendentally situated.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Bhāgavatam affirms that any person who is fully in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, or devotional service of the Lord, has all the good qualities of the great sages, whereas a person who is not so transcendentally situated has no good qualifications, because he is sure to be taking refuge in his own mental concoctions.

O Partha, yada, when; prajahati, one fully renounces; sarvan, all; the kaman, desires, varieties of desires; manogatan, that have entered the mind. Tustah, remains satisfied; atmani eva, in the Self alone, in the very nature of the inmost Self; atmana, by the Self which is his own — indifferent to external gains, and satiated with everything else on account of having attained the nectar of realization of the supreme Goal; tada, then; ucyate, he is called; sthita-prajnah, a man of steady wisdom, a man of realization, one whose wisdom, arising from the discrimination between the Self and the not-Self, is stable.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the opening definition of the sthita-prajña: the one who fully renounces every desire that has entered the mind (manogatān) and remains satisfied in the Self (ātman) by the Self itself. The Advaita gloss anchors the contentment in non-dual Self-realization — the sage delights in the Self because the Self is not different from Brahman. Critically, Shankara warns that if desires are renounced while bodily life continues, the absence of satisfying objects could produce lunatic-like behaviour; the verse forestalls this by specifying that contentment is intrinsic — drawn from the Self, not the world.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Lord said — When a person is satisfied in himself with himself, i.e. when his mind depends on the self within himself; and being content with that, expels all the desires of the mind which are different from that state of mind — then he is said to be a man of firm wisdom. This is the highest form of devotion of knowledge.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads ātmany evātmanā tuṣṭaḥ as the highest state of jñāna-bhakti: the mind depends on the inner self, and from that dependence-content, expels every desire foreign to that state. The Vishishtadvaita reading frames the verse not as Self-as-Brahman absorption (Shankara) but as the highest stage of devotional knowledge — the inner self abiding in the Supreme.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Lord Krishna reveals the attributes of the spiritually intelligent in this verse. One situated in spiritual intelligence realises the soul by the grace of the Supreme Lord… In actuality such a one discards all desires. Even as in the case of Sukadeva and Dhruva after they had darshan or vision of the Supreme Lord. The spiritually intelligent always enthusiastically desire communion with the Supreme Lord.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Madhva's Dvaita reading anchors the verse in grace: the realisation of the soul occurs by the grace of the Supreme Lord, and from that realisation all worldly desires are discarded — though one then desires communion with the Lord. The contentment is drawn from the relationship with the Supreme, not from non-dual identity.

positions.rendering: his mind, thus purified, finds satisfaction in the self alone
becomes satisfied in the realization of the self
satisfied in the Self alone, by the Self which is his own — content with the inmost Self, indifferent to external gains
satisfied in himself with himself; the mind depends on the self within himself, and being content with that…
contentment drawn from realisation of the soul by the grace of the Supreme Lord; communion with the Supreme replaces worldly desires

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The verse's central phrase ātmany evātmanā tuṣṭaḥ is dense — 'satisfied in the Self by the Self' — and the three traditions read it through their respective metaphysics. Shankara reads ātman as the non-dual Self (= Brahman); contentment is intrinsic because the Self is the substrate of all. Ramanuja reads ātman as the individual self abiding in the Supreme; contentment is the highest state of jñāna-bhakti. Madhva reads it as the soul realised by grace; contentment is the relational content of communion with the Lord. The verse's grammatical form supports all three — the locative ātmani and instrumental ātmanā are simply the place and the means of contentment, with the metaphysical scope determined by the reader's frame.

positions.rendering: all varieties of desire for sense gratification, which arise from mental concoction
all selfish desires and cravings of the senses that torment the mind
all desires, varieties of desires, that have entered the mind / heart

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: manogatān literally means 'gone-into-mind' — desires already lodged in the mind. Prabhupada glosses 'arise from mental concoction'; Mukundananda glosses 'cravings that torment the mind'; Shankara reads it most literally as 'desires that have entered the mind / heart'. The literal sense is sharpest: not desires that exist in the abstract, but the ones already established in the operator's mind. The verse asks for the renunciation of those, not just the pre-emption of new ones.

literal_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: When one fully abandons all desires that have entered the mind, O Partha, content in the Self by the Self alone, then he is called the steady-wisdomed one.
traditional_meaning_consensus: This is Krishna's first answer to Arjuna's question of 2.54. The sthita-prajña is defined by two coordinated marks: (1) the abandonment of all desires that have entered the mind (kāmān manogatān prajahāti) — not just future desires, but the ones already present; (2) contentment in the Self by the Self (ātmany evātmanā tuṣṭaḥ) — an intrinsic source of satisfaction that does not depend on external supply. Shankara reads contentment as Self-as-Brahman absorption; Ramanuja reads it as the highest stage of devotional knowledge in which the inner self abides in the Supreme; Madhva reads it as relational communion with the Lord, granted by grace. All three agree on the structural point: the source of contentment is not what the world supplies, but what is found within. The verse names the operator-state, not just the operator's actions; the next verses will give the behavioural marks.

remaining_gaps: Shankara's full Sanskrit bhāṣya not pulled — only translator-rendered excerpt was machine-extractable.
